sat_relevance: Relevant when SAT concentration or intensity is explained by water regulation.
topic: mild water stress, vine regulation, and grape concentration

steps.label: cause
steps.text: Mild water stress occurs when a vine has limited but not critically scarce access to water during the ripening period, often on free-draining soils or under controlled (e.g. drip) irrigation in dry climates.

steps.label: mechanism
steps.text: Limited water causes the vine to slow shoot and canopy growth and divert resources to the berries; berry size stays small, raising the skin-to-juice ratio, while sugar, anthocyanin and flavour compounds concentrate. Severe stress, by contrast, shuts ripening down.

steps.label: effect
steps.text: Mild water stress tends to produce smaller berries with greater concentration of flavour, colour and tannin and higher quality potential; excessive irrigation or rainfall dilutes these and can reduce quality.

_meta.source_note: Inferred formative model — Phase P.2 batch-2 knowledge expansion. Not from official WSET mark scheme.
